whatItDoes: Convert docker run commands to Docker Compose YAML files. Parses the command to extract image, ports, volumes, environment variables, network, restart policy, and container name, then generates a complete docker-compose.yml configuration.
whyItExists: Migrating from docker run commands to Docker Compose requires manually translating flags into YAML syntax, which is error-prone; this tool automates the conversion and provides a parsed breakdown of all components.
whoShouldUse: DevOps engineers, backend developers, and system administrators who are migrating from standalone Docker commands to Docker Compose orchestration.
useCases: Convert a complex docker run command with many flags into clean docker-compose.yml
Migrate a deployment script from docker run to docker-compose up
Quickly generate compose configuration for a container with specific port mappings
Understand how docker run flags map to Docker Compose YAML properties
Generate compose files for multi-container applications
Preview the parsed components before generating the YAML output
instructions: Paste your docker run command into the input textarea
The tool parses all flags and displays the extracted components
Review the parsed components: image, ports, volumes, env vars, network, restart policy
Copy the generated docker-compose.yml YAML to your clipboard
The YAML includes a networks section if a custom network was specified
examples: docker run -d -p 8080:80 -e NODE_ENV=prod nginx → service with port mapping and env var
docker run --name db -v /data:/var/lib/postgresql/data postgres:14 → service with named volume
docker run --network backend --restart always redis → service with custom network and restart policy
bestPractices: Review the generated YAML before using it in production — some flags may need manual adjustment
Add health checks, resource limits, and logging configuration that docker run doesn't capture
Use named volumes instead of bind mounts for better portability
Consider adding a .env file for environment variables instead of hardcoding them
Test the generated compose file with docker-compose config before deploying
commonMistakes: Forgetting to map all ports — complex port mappings may not parse correctly
Not adding health checks in the compose file that were implicit in the docker run setup
Assuming all docker run flags have direct compose equivalents
Using the generated YAML without reviewing it for correctness
Missing environment variables that were passed via --env-file instead of -e
faq: Does this handle all docker run flags? — It handles the most common flags: -p, -v, -e, --name, --network, --restart, --rm, -d. Some advanced flags may not be parsed.
Can I convert docker-compose.yml back to docker run? — This tool is one-way only. For reverse conversion, consider using docker-compose config.
What about multi-stage builds? — This tool only handles runtime configuration. Build-related settings need to be added manually.
Does the generated YAML include version numbers? — Yes, it uses version 3.8 which is compatible with most Docker Compose installations.
